content:
**DBSCAN** was introduced by **Ester**, **Kriegel**, **Sander**, and **Xu** in their 1996 paper *A Density-Based Algorithm for Discovering Clusters in Large Spatial Databases with Noise* (KDD-96). It responded to limitations of **partitioning** methods like **k-means** and **CLARANS** that assumed spherical clusters and required **k** upfront, and could not mark outliers explicitly.

The key idea — define clusters as **maximal sets of density-connected points** and treat sparse points as **noise** — unified **cluster discovery** and **outlier detection** in one pass over **ε-neighborhood** graphs.

**OPTICS** (1999) generalized ordering of points by reachability distance for **interactive** eps exploration without refitting from scratch.

**HDBSCAN** (2013) extended hierarchy-based **varying-density** clustering, addressing DBSCAN's single-eps weakness while preserving noise semantics.

DBSCAN became a default in **spatial databases**, **GIS**, and **scikit-learn** (`sklearn.cluster.DBSCAN`) as a practical density baseline alongside k-means and hierarchical methods.

timeline.label: DBSCAN paper (1996)
timeline.description: Ester et al. formalize core/border/noise points and density-connected clusters for spatial databases with noise.

timeline.label: OPTICS (1999)
timeline.description: Ankerst et al. produce reachability ordering to explore multiple eps values from one run.

timeline.label: Library adoption (2000s)
timeline.description: DBSCAN integrated into R, WEKA, and later scikit-learn as a standard density clustering option.

timeline.label: HDBSCAN (2013)
timeline.description: Campello et al. extend hierarchical density methods for clusters of varying density while retaining noise labels.

timeline.label: Modern ML stacks (2010s–present)
timeline.description: DBSCAN remains a go-to for geospatial hotspots, anomaly pre-screening, and non-convex cluster baselines when eps is tunable.
